data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0852 GMT - Germany's DAX rises in morning trade to reach a new record intraday high in a session with modest but broad-based gains across major European stock indexes. The DAX is up 0.4% at 24,366, having risen as high as 24,399.03, climbing past the previous intraday high of 23,346.15 set Wednesday. This marks the German blue-chip index's 30th all-time high this year, according to a tally by HSBC. Bayer, Heidelberg Materials and Daimler Truck Holding top the DAX gainers. Elsewhere, the Stoxx Europe 600 rises 0.3%, and the main indexes in the U.K., France, Spain, Italy, Switzerland and the Netherlands are all up. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0849 GMT - Wise's plan to dual list shares in the U.S. and the U.K., with the intention to change the primary listing to a U.S. stock exchange, should be positive for shares, UBS analysts Justin Forsythe and Michael Briest say in a research note. However, the bull case for FTSE 100 inclusion isn't on the table if the money-transfer company isn't pursuing a London Stock Exchange commercial listing, the analysts say. "We understand this was not possible due to private markets investors holding super voting B shares," UBS says. Shares trade 9.1% higher at 11.82 pounds. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0833 GMT - Wizz Air's costs are offsetting the company's positive pricing which will lead to consensus downgrades, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Though the low-cost airline says its summer performance shows positive revenue from each plane seat available for sale, it is also expecting slightly higher costs per available plane seat flown. This increase comes at a time when the market was expecting those costs to come down, the analysts say. The rise is driven by higher airport and maintenance costs, and higher depreciation costs related to grounded aircraft, the analyst say. JPMorgan says it expects fiscal 2026 net income consensus to decline by about 20% to 30% from the current expectation of 335 million euros. Shares trade 23% down at 1,284 pence. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0829 GMT - Bayer's strong start to the year and signs of a recovery in its pharmaceutical business suggests earnings expectations might have bottomed out, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a research note. Consensus earnings estimates on the German agricultural and pharma group could start to trend higher as the market gives credit to its cost-savings plan, the analysts say. Moreover, the risk-reward equation on litigation updates through 2025 looks more favorable, according to Goldman Sachs. Legal cases over Bayer's Roundup weedkiller have been a drag on its stock since it lost the first trial in 2018, the analysts say. Goldman Sachs raises its recommendation on Bayer to buy from neutral, and lifts its target price to 33 euros from 29.10 euros. Shares jump 4.9% to 26.55 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0828 GMT - BP continues to expect that half of its target to boost free cash flow 20% by 2027 will be achieved in 2025, Citi analysts write. The analysts attended investor meetings with the energy giant's finance chief. The boost is due to better operational performance of the Whiting refinery, a full-year contribution from its biofuels division Bunge, and some cost-improvement gains, they write. This improvement is likely to be weighted toward the second half of the year, the analysts add. Shares trade up 0.2% at 359.35 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0821 GMT - Fevertree Drinks keeping guidance despite the 10% U.S. tariffs on U.K. exports is a reassuringly positive message, Panmure Liberum analysts Anubhav Malhotra and Wayne Brown say in a note. The company will share the cost of tariffs equally with Molson Coors as part of their partnership arrangement as they work to mitigate their short-term effect. "A positive message as we believe tariffs could have had an unmitigated impact of as high as around 3 million pounds in 2025, equivalent to around 80bps of margin for the group," the analysts say. Shares are down 0.2% at 882 pence, and are up 31% in the year-to-date. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0821 GMT - While Wise's in-line figures and backing of full-year guidance isn't surprising, its announcement to move its primary listing to the U.S. is, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. Although the money-transfer company will remain dual-listed in the U.K., Wise aims to tap a broader pool of growth investors. Any share movement is likely driven by this announcement, the analysts say. Shares trade 8.8% higher at 11.78 pounds.(nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0809 GMT - CMC Group's earnings were better than RBC Capital Markets had expected, analysts Ben Bathurst and Jude Neanor write. While the London-listed trading platform didn't provide formal guidance, it did comment on momentum continuing into the current fiscal year. As a result, the analysts upgrade their earnings-per-share estimates by 26% and 23% for fiscal 2026 and 2027, respectively. They also increase the stock's target price by 9% to 380 pence from 350 pence. Shares trade down 13% at 247.5 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0807 GMT - European natural gas prices rise in early trade. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is up 1.9% to 36.34 euros a megawatt hour as traders focus on replenishing storage ahead of the winter. Supply tightened as flows from Norway--Europe's largest single supplier--declined due to a new round of planned maintenance. At the same time, forecasts of warm weather in Asia have raised the prospect of increased competition for liquefied natural gas. "Above normal temperatures are expected across China and Japan, the two biggest buyers of LNG, over the next weeks," analysts at ANZ Research say. "Higher air-conditioning requirements or reduced hydro output could boost gas needs." Investors are also monitoring geopolitical developments, with little progress in peace talks to end the war in Ukraine and persistent trade tensions. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0806 GMT - BP's sale of Castrol is now in its second phase and the U.K. energy company has signed a number of nondisclosure agreements with interested parties, Citi analysts write. The analysts spent a day with BP's finance chief Kate Thomson in investor meetings. BP might launch the sell down of LightSource BP by the end of the second quarter, they add. The company said there is a credible pathway to hitting debt-reduction targets in 2026 via asset sales, they say. Shares trade up 0.2% at 359.35 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - Wizz Air's operating profit for fiscal 2025 missed analysts' expectations, RBC Capital Markets analyst Ruairi Cullinane says in a research note. The result is likely to lead to consensus downgrades for the company's earnings for the current fiscal year. Given the low-cost airline has guided for an increase in unit costs excluding fuel, consensus is expected to settle in the 200 million to 270 million-euro range from 335 million euros previously, the analyst says. The company didn't provide guidance for fiscal 2026 due to lack of visibility. Wizz Air shares trade 24% down at 12.66 pounds. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0756 GMT - Oil prices edge higher after slipping around 1% in the previous session amid persistent concerns over excess supply and softening demand. Brent crude gains 0.4% to $65.11 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.3% at $63.04 a barrel. A bigger-than-expected drawdown in U.S. crude inventories was offset by builds in gasoline and diesel stocks, reflecting weaker demand in the world's top oil consumer. Meanwhile, fears of a supply glut continue to weigh on sentiment after Bloomberg reported Saudi Arabia is seeking another big production increase at next month's OPEC+ meeting. "This strategic move aims to capitalize on the peak oil demand traditionally observed during the Northern Hemisphere's summer season, a period when fuel consumption typically surges due to travel and economic activity," says Antonio Di Giacomo, analyst at XS.com. Separately, Aramco lowered its selling price to Asia, suggesting demand is tepid. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Apple to Boost STMicroelectronics Revenue in 2Q — Market Talk
data.description: STMicroelectronics' sales of chips for personal electronics are expected to get a boost from Apple in the second quarter, Jefferies analysts write in a research note. CEO Jean-Marc Chery said at an event hosted by BNP Paribas that the company expects to exceed its forecasts for personal-electronics revenue thanks to its biggest client, Apple. The iPhone maker accounted for 14.5% of STMicroelectronics' total revenue last year, according to the company's 2024 financial report. STMicroelectronics shares in Paris trade 0.1% lower at 24.90 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Ariana Resources Says Cold-commissioning Started at Tavsan Gold Mine's Processing Plant in Turkey
data.description:
Ariana Resources said Thursday that cold-commissioning commenced at the processing plant of the Tavsan gold mine in Türkiye.

The cold-commissioning is expected to be completed in stages through June, with the plant to be fully operational in July.

Gold-focused mineral exploration and development company Ariana owns a 23.5% stake in mine owner Zenit Madencilik, along with Ozaltin Holding and Proccea Construction.

data.title: Formycon Wins Brazilian Regulatory Approval for Lucentis Biosimilar
data.description:
Formycon said Thursday it secured Brazilian regulatory authority approval for Ranivisio, its biosimilar to Lucentis, which is used in treating severe visual impairments.

The German biosimilar developer's local partner Biomm plans to launch the product in the fourth quarter, marking the start of its commercial rollout across Latin America.

Ranivisio has already been approved in Peru, El Salvador, Honduras, and the Dominican Republic. Formycon said it expects additional market authorizations and product launches throughout Central and South America by early 2027.

data.title: Fire Rock Plans Indonesia Expansion with HK$60 Million Investment
data.description:
Fire Rock said it plans to expand into Indonesia as its third key Southeast Asian market, following Singapore and Thailand, according to a Wednesday Hong Kong bourse filing.

Through its British Virgin Islands-based unit Goodvibe International, the group will set up a research and development team and publish games in Indonesia.

It also intends to explore acquisitions or investments in interactive internet projects.

Fire Rock said it plans to allocate about HK$60 million as funding for its development in Southeast Asia for the next three years.

The Indonesian business is expected to begin building its R&D team in H2 2025 and start generating revenue by mid-2026. The company currently has no operations or income from Indonesia, the filing said.

Shares of Fire Rock closed about 3% higher on Thursday.

data.title: Market Chatter: Kimberly-Clark Nears $3.5 Billion Sale of International Tissue Business to Suzano
data.description:
Kimberly-Clark is close to selling its Kleenex and tissue businesses outside of North America to Brazil's Suzano for about $3.5 billion, The Wall Street Journal reported Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The transaction could be completed as soon as Thursday, according to the report.

Kimberly-Clark could not be reached by MT Newswires for comment, while Suzano didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Suzano shares were down 1.5% in recent premarket activity Thursday.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Bittium Enters ECG Monitoring Device Deal with Boston Scientific
data.description:
Bittium unit Bittium Biosignals agreed to deliver its electrocardiogram, or ECG, measuring devices for monitoring cardiac arrhythmias to Boston Scientific.

The total value of the deal is estimated at between $30 million and $45 million, depending on the agreement's scope and implementation. Revenue from the agreement will be recognized gradually between 2025 and 2028, the Finnish communication technology group said Thursday.

Bittium Biosignals and Boston Scientific will also continue the joint development of customized ECG technologies as part of the collaboration.

data.title: AMSEC initiates India's HAL with 'reduce'; flags execution risks
data.description:
** Asian Markets Securities starts coverage on Hindustan Aeronautics with "reduce"; TP 4,750 rupees, 4% discount to last close

** Says optimism on HAL's strong order pipeline already priced into stock but risks of timely execution remain key

** Adds, despite high order inflows, HAL forecast revenue growth of ~8%-10% in FY26

** Forecast dependent on receiving engines from General Electri c as "so far there's been a significant delay as per original schedule," says AMSEC

** Last few years' revenue CAGR (FY19-25) stood at ~8% despite ~21% growth in order book - AMSEC

** If deliveries are timely and co generates ~27% margins, then revenue CAGR growth could be ~23% over FY25-FY27

** Despite this, HAL up 1.3% higher on Thursday

** Stock rated "buy" on avg, median PT 5,645 rupees - data compiled by LSEG

** Shares up 20% YTD

data.title: S&P: UK's May Construction PMI Inches Up but Remains in Contraction Territory
data.description:
The British construction downturn eased in May as both output and new orders declined at the softest rate since January, while the growth outlook improved, S&P Global said Thursday.

The S&P Global UK Construction PMI came in at 47.9, up from 46.6 in April and the consensus estimate of 47.2.

data.title: PROJECTS: ACWA Power allocates majority of spending from rights issue on MENA projects
data.description:
Staff Writer

ACWA Power has allocated 53 percent to 60 percent of the proceeds from its 7.12 billion Saudi riyals ($1.9 billion) rights issue for spending in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA).

The company expects to spend between SAR 3.7 billion and SAR 4.2 billion on current and future projects in the MENA region, the Saudi-listed company stated in its rights issue prospectus.

The expenditure for Central Asia is expected to be between 22 percent and 25 percent, or SAR 1.5 billion to SAR 1.7 billion.

Therefore, the total spend on renewable energy, water and gas projects will reach between SAR 5.2 billion and SAR 5.9 billion, the prospectus said.

ACWA Power anticipates allocating a maximum of 20 percent of the proceeds, amounting to SAR 1.4 billion, for mergers and acquisitions.

The potential allocation for general corporate purposes will reach a maximum of SAR 352 million.

As of December 31, 2024, ACWA Power’s portfolio comprises 94 projects in 13 countries, with a total investment cost of SAR 364.5 billion ($97.32 billion).

The projects are a mix of renewable energy and storage, water desalination, green hydrogen, and flexible generation (conventional fuel).

According to the prospectus, 52 projects are in operation at a total investment cost of SAR 196.9 billion, 24 are under construction valued at SAR 109.5 billion and 18 are in the advanced development stage worth SAR 58.1 billion.

The company clarified that advanced development stage projects refer to ones where a preferred bidder has been selected, a long-term offtake agreement has been signed, or deals have been negotiated, where it has committed significant financial resources and reaching financial close is at an advanced stage.

The company's shareholders will vote on the board of directors’ recommendation to increase the capital through a SAR 7.12 billion rights issue on 30 June 2025.

(Editing by Anoop Menon) (anoop.menon@lseg.com)

data.title: Wise's Intention to Dual List Shares Is Positive — Market Talk
data.description: Wise's plan to dual list shares in the U.S. and the U.K., with the intention to change the primary listing to a U.S. stock exchange, should be positive for shares, UBS analysts Justin Forsythe and Michael Briest say in a research note. However, the bull case for FTSE 100 inclusion isn't on the table if the money-transfer company isn't pursuing a London Stock Exchange commercial listing, the analysts say. "We understand this was not possible due to private markets investors holding super voting B shares," UBS says. Shares trade 9.1% higher at 11.82 pounds. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

data.title: More Interest Rate Cuts By Major Central Banks Could Boost Equities, Weaken Currencies — Market Talk
data.description:
0849 GMT - Additional interest rate cuts by major central banks will have a significant impact on financial markets, XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. Rate cuts are likely to boost equities and weaken the currencies of those countries. "It could feed risk sentiment and keep equity markets elevated for the foreseeable future." It could result in "a race to the bottom" for major currencies, she says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0831 GMT - Singapore's retail sales are likely to remain tepid this year, UOB associate economist Jester Koh writes in a note. Retail sales have remained sluggish year-to-date, likely due to the slow recovery in tourist arrivals, he says. The recovery of Chinese tourists appears to be slowing, possibly reflecting a shift toward domestic tourism, Koh adds. Retail sales are also affected by the diversion of resident spending abroad, with a relatively strong Singapore dollar compared to Asian currencies year-to-date, Koh says, adding that Budget 2025 measures and tourism promotion efforts may boost retail sales activity. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0756 GMT - Oil prices edge higher after slipping around 1% in the previous session amid persistent concerns over excess supply and softening demand. Brent crude gains 0.4% to $65.11 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.3% at $63.04 a barrel. A bigger-than-expected drawdown in U.S. crude inventories was offset by builds in gasoline and diesel stocks, reflecting weaker demand in the world's top oil consumer. Meanwhile, fears of a supply glut continue to weigh on sentiment after Bloomberg reported Saudi Arabia is seeking another big production increase at next month's OPEC+ meeting. "This strategic move aims to capitalize on the peak oil demand traditionally observed during the Northern Hemisphere's summer season, a period when fuel consumption typically surges due to travel and economic activity," says Antonio Di Giacomo, analyst at XS.com. Separately, Aramco lowered its selling price to Asia, suggesting demand is tepid. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0756 GMT - Amid increasing economic uncertainty, DBS continues to expect downside risks to Singapore's retail sales over the coming months. Retail sales growth slowed to a sluggish 0.3% on year in April, compared with a revised 1.3% in March. Weaker domestic labor-market conditions may further dampen household spending and retail sales, says senior economist Chua Han Teng in an email. Uncertainty in global trade policy and elevated trade tensions will negatively hurt Singapore's export-reliant economy, especially in 2H, he adds. This would weigh on business hiring and wage increment, leading to belt-tightening by households and pressuring retail sales, Chua says.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0754 GMT - The Philippine central bank could hold rates in June and ease in August and 4Q, Barclays economist Shreya Sodhani writes in a note. BSP Gov. Eli Remolona recently noted that the central bank could cut rates twice before the year ends, but not necessarily consecutively. There are downside risks to growth and inflation, suggesting that more rate cuts are needed, Sodhani notes. However, she says that the Federal Reserve's path should play an equally important role in determining the magnitude of rate cuts by the BSP. Barclays U.S. economists expect the Fed to deliver one more cut this year, in December. "This gives the BSP further reason to stay on hold when it announces its decisions half a day after the Fed's June meeting," Sodhani says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0748 GMT - A recovery in German industry remains underway, ING macro analyst Carsten Brzeski says, after orders to the country's factories unexpectedly rose at the start of the second quarter. Rising order books over the previous three months had been pinned to a rush from U.S. importers to stock up ahead of tariffs, but that now looks only part of the story. Foreign orders retreated only slightly in April and domestic demand proved robust. "The feared full reversal of the frontloading effect of the first quarter has not materialised," Brzeski says. "Instead, it looks as if the turning of the German industrial cycle is continuing." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0742 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall, tracking declines in their U.S. and eurozone equivalents. It follows Wednesday's weaker-than-expected U.S. labor market data and ISM services index. These data raised confidence that the Fed would still cut interest rates this year, Deutsche Bank Research strategists say in a note. Investors also await a European Central Bank rate decision due at 1215 GMT, where an interest rate cut is widely expected. The 10-year gilt yields fall 2 basis points to 4.600%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0735 GMT - South Korea's new Democratic Party government could face challenges in tariff talks with the U.S. if the Trump administration seeks to weaken Korean-Chinese trade linkages, Fitch Ratings analysts write in a note. The Democratic Party has historically sought balance in the country's ties with the U.S. and China, they say. They also see a lot of uncertainty over U.S. trade policy. South Korea is subject to a 25% reciprocal tariff that Trump has proposed but temporarily paused for negotiations. The U.S. may impose new levies on semiconductors, a major South Korean export item, in addition to current duties of 25% on autos and 50% on steel, Fitch adds. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0729 GMT - If the European Central Bank reduces its inflation forecasts significantly when it announces a policy decision, it will likely limit the euro's gains against a weaker dollar, XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. The ECB is widely expected to cut interest rates by 25 basis points, taking the deposit rate to 2.0%. Recent weak eurozone inflation data mean inflation forecasts are also expected to be reduced. "A large downward revision to the ECB's inflation forecasts could limit the euro's upside against the dollar in the short term," Brooks says. The euro has gained ground against the dollar over the past month, despite European bond yields lagging the U.S., she says. The euro rises 0.1% to $1.1423. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0715 GMT - The U.S. dollar edges slightly higher against a basket of currencies in early European trade but remains weak and close to six-week lows. The dollar fell on Wednesday after weak U.S. ADP private payrolls data, while trade-policy uncertainty also weighs on the U.S. currency. "Interestingly, the U.S. dollar is one of the few assets reflecting trade pessimism," says Swissquote Bank's Ipek Ozkardeskaya in a note. The DXY index rises 0.1% to 98.849, having hit a six-week low of 98.583 earlier this week. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0702 GMT - A deceleration in Philippines' headline inflation to 1.3% in May from 1.4% the previous month raises the risk of a policy rate cut this month, says Aris Dacanay of HSBC Global Research. The peso's relative strength could also give Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas room to lower policy rates, the economist says in a note. That said, the June 19 meeting remains a tough call as upside risks to inflation remain. Dacanay points to the lower house of Congress's approval of an across-the-board minimum-wage hike this week. Policymakers are also considering raising tariff rates on rice. If this materializes, BSP may need to reconsider the pace of its easing cycle, he adds. HSBC's baseline scenario is for the central bank to pause its easing cycle in June. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0658 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening slightly lower. IG calls the OMXS30 down 0.1% at around 2508. Major stock exchanges were somewhat mixed on Wednesday, SEB analysts say in a note. Weak U.S. economic data increased expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut its policy rate in the coming months. Stock markets in Asia have moved in slightly different directions overnight and futures are close to zero in both the U.S. and Europe. ECB policymakers are expected to lower on Thursday and SEB doesn't expect the central bank to commit to a given rate path ahead. OMXS30 closed at 2510.47, OMXN40 at 2413.70 and OBX at 1499.55. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Taiwan's Foreign Exchange Reserves Rise to $592.95 Billion at May-End
data.description:
Taiwan's foreign exchange reserves stood at $592.95 billion as of the end of May, up by $10.12 million from a month earlier, the central bank announced Thursday.

The rise is attributable to income from investment of foreign exchange reserves, exchange rate movements of other reserve currencies against the greenback, and market adjustments made by the central bank to maintain order in the foreign exchange market.

data.title: Xinjiang Lixin Energy to Build Three Projects in China's Xinjiang Region
data.description:
Xinjiang Lixin Energy plans to build three energy storage projects in China's Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region through its subsidiaries, according to a Shenzhen bourse filing on Thursday.

The energy services provider will build a 100-megawatt/400 megawatt-hour energy storage in Hotan City for 258 million yuan, 200MW/800MWh energy storage in Minfeng County for 515 million yuan, and the phase II 200MW/800MWh energy storage in Pishan County for 477 million yuan.

The subsidiaries, Hotan City Lixin Integrated Energy, Minfeng County Lixin Integrated Energy, and Pishan County Lixin Integrated Energy, will manage the 12-month construction of each project.

The subsidiaries received project filing certificates from the Hotan Regional Development and Reform Commission for the projects, the filing said.

The company's shares closed 10% higher.
